perturbation.original_text: PFHOF shall, within fourteen (14) days of its receipt of the recommendation of the Advisory Board, either approve or reject the proposal as set forth in the Notice. If PFHOF does not approve the proposal as set forth in the Notice within fourteen (14) days of PFHOF's receipt of the recommendation of the Advisory Board, such proposal shall be deemed rejected by PFHOF.
perturbation.changed_text: PFHOF shall, within ten (10) days of its receipt of the recommendation of the Advisory Board, either approve or reject the proposal as set forth in the Notice. If PFHOF does not approve the proposal as set forth in the Notice within ten (10) days of PFHOF's receipt of the recommendation of the Advisory Board, such proposal shall be deemed rejected by PFHOF.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the approval deadline from 14 to 10 days might create issues depending on the specific requirements of Ohio law regarding contract review periods. While there isn't a specific law mandating a 14-day review, reducing the time could be argued as 'unconscionable' if it doesn't provide sufficient time for a thorough review, particularly for complex proposals. Ohio law generally requires a reasonable time for contract review, and 10 days may not always be considered reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1302.15 addresses unconscionable contract or clause. If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made, the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. Reducing the review period from 14 to 10 days could be deemed unconscionable if it deprives a party of reasonable time to understand and assess complex terms, particularly given the nature of the media rights being considered.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1302.15 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1302.15 | Unconscionable contract or clause - UCC 2-302. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1302 Sales Effective: July 1, 1962 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 5 - 104th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (B) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Available Versions of this Section July 1, 1962 – Senate Bill 5 - 104th General Assembly [ View July 1, 1962 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The change from 14 days to 10 days for proposal approval could be deemed unconscionable under Ohio Revised Code § 1302.15, as it may deprive a party of a reasonable opportunity to understand and assess complex terms. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating, 'If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract.' The reduction in time, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation, might not allow for a 'reasonable time' for review, potentially making the clause unenforceable due to unconscionability.

perturbation.original_text: Thereafter, the agreement shall automatically renew for successive five (5)-year terms, unless either Party gives written notice to the other Party of intent not to renew at least six (6) months prior to the expiration of the then-current Term.
perturbation.changed_text: Thereafter, the agreement shall automatically renew for successive five (5)-year terms, unless either Party gives written notice to the other Party of intent not to renew at least two (2) months prior to the expiration of the then-current Term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notice period from 6 months to 2 months might be problematic under Ohio law, specifically if it's considered unreasonable and creates an undue burden on one party. While Ohio doesn't have a statute mandating a specific notice period for contract renewals, courts can intervene if a clause is deemed unconscionable or against public policy. A short notice period, especially in a complex agreement, could be challenged.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1302.15, while primarily focused on sales, embodies the broader principle of unconscionability in contracts. A two-month notice period for non-renewal of a complex, long-term media license agreement could be argued as unconscionable if it doesn't provide sufficient time for the other party to make alternative arrangements or mitigate potential losses, especially considering the significant investments and collaborations involved.

(B) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for non-renewal from six months to two months. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this change could be deemed unconscionable under Ohio law, potentially contradicting the principle outlined in Ohio Revised Code § 1302.15, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1. This snippet states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable. The reduced notice period might not provide sufficient time for the other party to make alternative arrangements, potentially resulting in an unconscionable outcome, especially given the complex nature of media license agreements.

perturbation.original_text: provided that the non-breaching Party provides prior written notice to the breaching Party, specifying the alleged material breach, and further provided that the breaching Party shall have thirty (30) days after receipt of such notice to cure the material breach, to the reasonable satisfaction of the non-breaching Party; provided, further, that if such breach (other than a breach for non-payment) cannot be cured during such 30-day period, but the allegedly breaching Party has commenced and is continuing good faith efforts to cure such breach within such 30-day period, then the cure period shall be extended until the allegedly breaching Party has stopped making good faith efforts to cure such breach, such extension not to exceed ninety (90) days.
perturbation.changed_text: provided that the non-breaching Party provides prior written notice to the breaching Party, specifying the alleged material breach, and further provided that the breaching Party shall have ten (10) days after receipt of such notice to cure the material breach, to the reasonable satisfaction of the non-breaching Party; provided, further, that if such breach (other than a breach for non-payment) cannot be cured during such 10-day period, but the allegedly breaching Party has commenced and is continuing good faith efforts to cure such breach within such 10-day period, then the cure period shall be extended until the allegedly breaching Party has stopped making good faith efforts to cure such breach, such extension not to exceed thirty (30) days.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from 30 days (with a possible 90-day extension for good faith efforts) to 10 days (with a possible 30-day extension) could be considered unreasonable under Ohio law, particularly if the breach involves complex issues requiring more time to resolve. While Ohio law doesn't dictate specific cure periods, courts can deem a clause unconscionable if it doesn't provide a fair opportunity to remedy the breach. A shorter period might be seen as oppressive, especially for breaches requiring significant effort to correct.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1302.15 allows courts to refuse enforcement of unconscionable clauses. Reducing the cure period to 10 days, with a maximum 30-day extension, may be viewed as unconscionable if the nature of potential breaches under this media license agreement (e.g., failure to properly exploit rights, disputes over licensing fees) often require more than 10 or even 30 days to remedy due to complexities in media production, distribution, and financial reconciliation.

(B) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Available Versions of this Section July 1, 1962 – Senate Bill 5 - 104th General Assembly [ View July 1, 1962 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period for a breach from 30 days to 10 days, with a corresponding reduction in the extension period. This directly contradicts the principle outlined in Ohio Revised Code § 1302.15, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which allows courts to refuse enforcement of unconscionable contract clauses. The Law Explanation argues that the reduced cure period may be viewed as unconscionable, especially given the complexities involved in media license agreements. While the snippet doesn't specify the length of a reasonable cure period, it grants the court the power to determine unconscionability, and a significantly reduced period could be deemed unreasonable, thus creating a legal contradiction as the modified term could be unenforceable.
